title: Check bipartite BFS
description: Check if graph is bipartite using BFS coloring

examples.explanation: Step 1: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). colors[0]=0. DFS(0,0): neighbors [1,3]. Vertex 1 unvisited: DFS(1,1): colors[1]=1; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 2 unvisited: DFS(2,0): colors[2]=0; neighbors [1,3]. Vertex 1 visited with color 1 ≠ 0 (ok). Vertex 3 unvisited: DFS(3,1): colors[3]=1; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 2 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Return True. Vertex 3 returns True. Vertex 2 returns True. Vertex 1 returns True. Vertex 0 returns True. All vertices visited, no conflicts. Output True.

Step 1: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0: colors[0]=0. DFS(0,0): neighbors [1,2,3]. Vertex 1 unvisited: DFS(1,1): colors[1]=1; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 2 unvisited: DFS(2,0): colors[2]=0; neighbors [0,1,3]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 = 0 (conflict!). Return False. Vertex 1 returns False. Vertex 0 returns False. Output False.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use depth-first search to recursively assign colors to vertices, checking for conflicts with adjacent vertices. It explores all possible color assignments but can be inefficient for large graphs due to recursion overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Bipartite Graph Using DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph is bipartite using DFS coloring approach.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph is one whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that no two vertices within the same set are adjacent. The DFS approach works by attempting to color the graph with two colors (say 0 and 1) such that adjacent vertices have different colors. The intuition is that if we can successfully color the entire graph without conflicts, it's bipartite; otherwise, it's not. We perform DFS from each unvisited vertex to handle disconnected graphs. During DFS, we assign the current vertex a color, then recursively visit all its neighbors. For each neighbor: if unvisited, assign the opposite color and continue DFS; if already visited, check if its color conflicts with the current vertex's color. A conflict means the graph isn't bipartite. The algorithm runs in O(V+E) time because each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the color array and recursion stack. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) as it orchestrates multiple DFS calls. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (need to check all components), forgetting to initialize colors properly, and incorrect conflict checking. Real code pattern: def isBipartite(graph): colors = [-1]*n; for v in range(n): if colors[v]==-1: if not dfs(v,0): return False; return True; def dfs(v,color): colors[v]=color; for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor]==-1: if not dfs(neighbor,1-color): return False; elif colors[neighbor]==color: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). colors[0]=0. DFS(0,0): neighbors [1,3]. Vertex 1 unvisited: DFS(1,1): colors[1]=1; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 2 unvisited: DFS(2,0): colors[2]=0; neighbors [1,3]. Vertex 1 visited with color 1 ≠ 0 (ok). Vertex 3 unvisited: DFS(3,1): colors[3]=1; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 2 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Return True. Vertex 3 returns True. Vertex 2 returns True. Vertex 1 returns True. Vertex 0 returns True. All vertices visited, no conflicts. Output True.

Step 1: colors=[-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0: colors[0]=0. DFS(0,0): neighbors [1,2,3]. Vertex 1 unvisited: DFS(1,1): colors[1]=1; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 2 unvisited: DFS(2,0): colors[2]=0; neighbors [0,1,3]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 = 0 (conflict!). Return False. Vertex 1 returns False. Vertex 0 returns False. Output False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Coloring Function
ladders.desc: Recursive DFS function that colors vertices and checks for conflicts with neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the bipartite checking algorithm. It performs a depth-first traversal from a given vertex, assigning colors and verifying the bipartite property locally. The intuition is that by recursively exploring all reachable vertices, we can propagate the two-coloring constraint through the graph. The algorithm: function dfs(vertex, color): assign 'color' to vertex; for each neighbor: if neighbor is unvisited, recursively call dfs(neighbor, opposite_color); if that returns false, propagate false upward; if neighbor is visited, check if its color equals current vertex's color—if yes, return false (conflict). If all neighbors pass, return true. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(V' + E') where V' and E' are vertices and edges in the connected component being processed. Space complexity is O(V') for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being called for each connected component. Common mistakes: not using the opposite color for recursive calls, incorrect conflict condition, or not propagating false returns properly. Real code pattern: def dfs(v,color): colors[v]=color; for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor]==-1: if not dfs(neighbor,1-color): return False; elif colors[neighbor]==color: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors[0]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Vertex 1 unvisited: dfs(1,1): colors[1]=1; neighbors [0,3]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 3 unvisited: dfs(3,0): colors[3]=0; neighbors [1,2]. Vertex 1 visited with color 1 ≠ 0 (ok). Vertex 2 unvisited: dfs(2,1): colors[2]=1; neighbors [0,3]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 3 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Return True. Vertex 3 returns True. Vertex 1 returns True. Back to vertex 0: neighbor 2 already visited during dfs(1) call, color 1 ≠ 0 (ok). All neighbors processed, return True.

Step 1: colors[0]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Vertex 1 unvisited: dfs(1,1): colors[1]=1; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 ≠ 1 (ok). Vertex 2 unvisited: dfs(2,0): colors[2]=0; neighbors [0,1]. Vertex 0 visited with color 0 = 0 (conflict!). Return False. Vertex 1 returns False. Vertex 0 returns False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Coloring Conflict Detection
ladders.desc: The central technique of checking if adjacent vertices have the same color during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental insight that makes bipartite checking possible: a graph is bipartite if and only if it can be 2-colored without adjacent vertices sharing the same color. The logic involves maintaining a color array where each vertex is assigned either 0 or 1. During traversal (DFS or BFS), when we encounter an edge between vertices u and v, we check: if v is unvisited, assign it the opposite color of u; if v is already visited, verify that its color is different from u's color. A conflict occurs when visited v has the same color as u, indicating an odd cycle that prevents bipartiteness. The intuition is that bipartite graphs contain no odd-length cycles—this coloring check detects such cycles. Time complexity for this logic per edge is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) extra per check. This feeds into L1 by being the condition inside the DFS loop. Common mistakes: comparing colors incorrectly (e.g., checking for equality when should check inequality), or not handling the case where a vertex is visited from a different path. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor]==-1: colors[neighbor]=1-color; else if colors[neighbor]==color: return False.

examples.input: Current vertex v=0 with color=0, neighbor=1 with color=-1 (unvisited)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check neighbor 1. Since colors[1] == -1, it's unvisited. Assign opposite color: colors[1] = 1 (since 1-0=1). No conflict, proceed to next neighbor or recursion.

examples.input: Current vertex v=0 with color=0, neighbor=1 with color=0 (already visited)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check neighbor 1. colors[1] == 0, which is equal to current vertex color 0. This is a conflict because adjacent vertices should have different colors in a bipartite graph. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with DFS
ladders.desc: Depth-first search to visit all vertices in a connected component of an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For an undirected graph, it visits all vertices reachable from a starting point. The intuition is to mimic exploring a maze: go deep into one path, mark visited locations, and backtrack when dead ends are reached. Algorithm: function dfs(vertex): mark vertex as visited; for each neighbor of vertex: if neighbor is not visited, recursively call dfs(neighbor). This ensures all vertices in the connected component are processed. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the component. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and visited set. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework where coloring logic is inserted. Common mistakes: not marking visited before recursion (causing infinite loops), or using incorrect graph representation. Real code pattern: def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for neighbor in graph[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Vertex 1 not visited: dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True; neighbors [0,3]. Vertex 0 visited (skip). Vertex 3 not visited: dfs(3): mark visited[3]=True; neighbors [1,2]. Vertex 1 visited (skip). Vertex 2 not visited: dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True; neighbors [0,3]. Both visited (skip). Return. Vertex 3 returns. Vertex 1 returns. Back to vertex 0: neighbor 2 already visited during dfs(1) call (skip). Traversal complete.

Step 1: Start at vertex 0, mark visited[0]=True. Neighbor: 1. Vertex 1 not visited: dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True; neighbors [0,2]. Vertex 0 visited (skip). Vertex 2 not visited: dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True; neighbors [1]. Vertex 1 visited (skip). Return. Vertex 1 returns. Vertex 0 returns. Traversal complete.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Graph Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Accessing and processing all neighbors of a vertex in an adjacency list representation.
ladders.explanation: This operation is fundamental to graph algorithms: given a vertex, we need to examine all vertices adjacent to it. In adjacency list representation, the graph is stored as an array of lists, where graph[i] contains all vertices connected to vertex i. Iterating involves looping through graph[v] to access each neighbor. The intuition is that graphs represent relationships, and processing neighbors allows traversing these relationships. Algorithm: for neighbor in graph[vertex]: process(neighbor). Time complexity is O(degree(v)) per vertex, where degree is the number of neighbors. Space complexity is O(1) extra per iteration. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism to explore edges during DFS. Common mistakes: using wrong index, modifying list during iteration, or assuming directedness in undirected graphs. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[v]: # do something with neighbor.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access graph[0] which is the list [1,2,3]. Iterate: first neighbor=1, process it; then neighbor=2, process; then neighbor=3, process. Complete after 3 iterations.
Step 1: Access graph[1] which is [0,2]. Iterate: first neighbor=0, process; then neighbor=2, process. Complete after 2 iterations.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation with Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Storing an undirected graph as a list of lists where each index corresponds to a vertex and its list contains connected vertices.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: how to represent a graph in code so we can perform algorithms on it. An adjacency list represents a graph as an array of linked lists or arrays. For an undirected graph with n vertices, we create a list 'graph' of size n, where graph[i] is a list containing all vertices j such that there is an edge between i and j. The intuition is that it efficiently stores connectivity information, using O(V+E) space. For example, if vertices are 0,1,2 and edges are (0,1) and (1,2), then graph = [[1],[0,2],[1]]. This representation allows quick access to neighbors. Time to access neighbors of vertex i is O(degree(i)). Space is O(V+E). This feeds into L4 as the data structure we iterate over. Common mistakes: not adding both directions for undirected edges (e.g., if edge (i,j), must add j to graph[i] and i to graph[j]), or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for edge in edges: graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]]. For edge (0,1): add 1 to graph[0] → graph[0]=[1]; add 0 to graph[1] → graph[1]=[0]. For edge (1,2): add 2 to graph[1] → graph[1]=[0,2]; add 1 to graph[2] → graph[2]=[1]. Final graph = [[1],[0,2],[1]].

approaches.name: BFS Coloring O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform breadth-first search to assign alternating colors (e.g., 0 and 1) to vertices level by level, checking for any adjacent vertices with the same color to detect non-bipartiteness. It ensures systematic exploration and is straightforward for bipartite checking.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bipartite Graph Detection via BFS Coloring
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph is bipartite using BFS with alternating colors.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph can be divided into two disjoint sets such that no two vertices within the same set are adjacent. The BFS coloring approach leverages the fact that in a bipartite graph, vertices at alternating levels of BFS traversal must have different colors. We perform BFS from each unvisited vertex (to handle disconnected graphs), assigning colors 0 and 1 alternately. If we encounter an adjacent vertex with the same color as the current vertex, the graph is not bipartite. The intuition is that BFS explores level by level, and in a bipartite graph, all edges connect vertices from different levels (colors). The algorithm runs in O(V+E) time as each vertex and edge is processed once, with O(V) space for color array and queue. This connects to L1 by integrating the BFS traversal with color checking. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs, forgetting to check all components, and incorrect color assignment logic. Real code pattern: Initialize color array with -1 (uncolored), iterate through vertices, if uncolored, start BFS, assign color 0, push to queue, while queue not empty, pop vertex, for each neighbor, if uncolored, assign opposite color and enqueue, else if same color, return false. Return true after all vertices processed.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0, color 0, queue = [0]. Step 2: Pop 0, neighbors 1 and 3. Color 1 = 1, color 3 = 1, queue = [1,3]. Step 3: Pop 1, neighbors 0 and 2. Color 0 is 0 (different), color 2 is uncolored, set to 0, queue = [3,2]. Step 4: Pop 3, neighbors 0 and 2. Color 0 is 0 (different), color 2 is 0 (different), queue = [2]. Step 5: Pop 2, neighbors 1 and 3. Color 1 is 1 (different), color 3 is 1 (different). All vertices colored, no conflicts, return true.

Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0, color 0, queue = [0]. Step 2: Pop 0, neighbors 1,2,3. Color 1 = 1, color 2 = 1, color 3 = 1, queue = [1,2,3]. Step 3: Pop 1, neighbors 0 and 2. Color 0 is 0 (different), color 2 is 1 (same as current color 1), conflict detected, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Color Assignment
ladders.desc: BFS function that traverses a connected component and assigns alternating colors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs BFS on a connected component of the graph, starting from a given vertex, and assigns colors 0 and 1 alternately to vertices. The intuition is that BFS explores vertices level by level, and in a bipartite graph, vertices at adjacent levels must have different colors. We use a queue to manage traversal: start by coloring the source vertex with 0, enqueue it, then while the queue is not empty, dequeue a vertex, and for each neighbor, if uncolored, assign the opposite color and enqueue it; if already colored, check if it has the same color as the current vertex—if so, return false (non-bipartite). This ensures that within a component, all edges connect vertices of different colors. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the component, space is O(V) for queue and color storage. This feeds into L0 by being called for each unvisited vertex to handle the entire graph. Common mistakes include not using a queue correctly, missing color checks for already colored neighbors, and incorrect opposite color calculation. Real code pattern: Define function bfs(start, colors, graph): queue = [start], colors[start] = 0, while queue: node = queue.pop(0), for neighbor in graph[node]: if colors[neighbor] == -1: colors[neighbor] = 1 - colors[node], queue.append(neighbor), elif colors[neighbor] == colors[node]: return false, return true.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: pop 0, neighbors 1 and 3. colors[1]=1, colors[3]=1, queue=[1,3]. Step 3: pop 1, neighbors 0 and 2. colors[0]=0 (different), colors[2]=0, queue=[3,2]. Step 4: pop 3, neighbors 0 and 2. colors[0]=0 (different), colors[2]=0 (different), queue=[2]. Step 5: pop 2, neighbors 1 and 3. colors[1]=1 (different), colors[3]=1 (different). Queue empty, return true.

Step 1: colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: pop 0, neighbors 1 and 2. colors[1]=1, colors[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. Step 3: pop 1, neighbors 0 and 2. colors[0]=0 (different), colors[2]=1 (same as current color 1), conflict detected, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Color Assignment in BFS
ladders.desc: The technique of assigning opposite colors to adjacent vertices during BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves assigning colors 0 and 1 to vertices such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color, which is the definition of a bipartite graph. During BFS, we traverse level by level: starting from a source vertex, we assign it color 0. As we visit neighbors, we assign them the opposite color (1 if current is 0, 0 if current is 1). This alternation ensures that vertices at the same BFS level have the same color, and vertices at adjacent levels have different colors. The intuition is that in a bipartite graph, edges only connect vertices from different sets (colors), so this assignment should hold without conflicts. If we find an edge connecting two vertices of the same color, the graph is not bipartite. This logic is applied iteratively in BFS. Time complexity per edge is O(1), space is O(1) for color variables. This feeds into L1 by being the central rule within the BFS traversal. Common mistakes include incorrect opposite color calculation (e.g., using 2 - color instead of 1 - color) and not checking colors before assignment. Real code pattern: For a vertex u with color c, for each neighbor v: if uncolored, set color[v] = 1 - c; else if color[v] == c, return false.

examples.trace: Step 1: Current vertex 0 has color 0. Step 2: For neighbor 1, uncolored, assign opposite color: 1 - 0 = 1. Step 3: For neighbor 2, uncolored, assign opposite color: 1 - 0 = 1. Result: vertices 1 and 2 colored 1.

examples.input: Current vertex 1 with color 1, neighbors [0,2] where colors are [0,1]
examples.trace: Step 1: Current vertex 1 has color 1. Step 2: Check neighbor 0, color is 0 (different, okay). Step 3: Check neighbor 2, color is 1 (same as current color 1), conflict detected, graph not bipartite.

testCases.expected: assign [1] to uncolored, check colored: 1 different
assign [0] to uncolored, check colored: 0 different
assign [1] to first, conflict at second
assign [0] to first, conflict at second

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Traversal of a Graph
ladders.desc: Perform breadth-first search on an undirected graph from a starting vertex.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that explores vertices level by level, starting from a source vertex. It uses a queue to manage the order of exploration: enqueue the start vertex, mark it as visited, then while the queue is not empty, dequeue a vertex, process it, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This ensures that vertices are visited in order of their distance from the source. The intuition is that BFS systematically covers all reachable vertices, making it ideal for checking properties like bipartiteness where level information is crucial. For an adjacency list representation, time complexity is O(V+E), and space is O(V) for the queue and visited set. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework where color assignment is integrated. Common mistakes include not using a queue (e.g., using recursion which is DFS), not marking vertices as visited when enqueuing (leading to duplicates), and not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*n, queue = [start], visited[start] = True, while queue: node = queue.pop(0), for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor] = True, queue.append(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, queue=[0], visited[0]=True. Step 2: Pop 0, neighbors 1 and 2, enqueue and mark visited: queue=[1,2], visited[1]=True, visited[2]=True. Step 3: Pop 1, neighbors 0 and 3, 0 visited, enqueue 3: queue=[2,3], visited[3]=True. Step 4: Pop 2, neighbors 0 and 3, both visited. Step 5: Pop 3, neighbors 1 and 2, both visited. Queue empty, traversal complete.

Step 1: Start at 0, queue=[0], visited[0]=True. Step 2: Pop 0, neighbor 1, enqueue and mark: queue=[1], visited[1]=True. Step 3: Pop 1, neighbors 0 and 2, 0 visited, enqueue 2: queue=[2], visited[2]=True. Step 4: Pop 2, neighbor 1 visited. Queue empty, traversal complete.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Management for BFS
ladders.desc: Using a queue to manage vertex traversal order in BFS.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) data structure essential for BFS to ensure vertices are processed in the order they are discovered. The basic operations are enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front). In BFS, we start by enqueuing the source vertex. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a vertex, process it, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This guarantees that vertices are explored level by level: all vertices at distance k from the source are processed before vertices at distance k+1. The intuition is that queues maintain the discovery order, which is critical for level-based algorithms like bipartite checking. Time complexity for queue operations is O(1) per enqueue/dequeue, and space is O(V) in worst case. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism that drives BFS traversal. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) instead of a queue, which would result in DFS, or not checking queue emptiness correctly. Real code pattern: Initialize empty list as queue, enqueue with append(), dequeue with pop(0) (or use collections.deque for efficiency).

examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue 0: queue = [0]. Step 2: Dequeue 0, process, enqueue neighbors 1 and 2: queue = [1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, process, no new neighbors (0 already visited): queue = [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, process, no new neighbors: queue = [].

Step 1: Enqueue 0: queue = [0]. Step 2: Dequeue 0, process, enqueue neighbor 1: queue = [1]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, process, enqueue neighbor 2: queue = [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, process, no new neighbors: queue = [].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Representing an undirected graph using a list of lists where each index corresponds to a vertex and its list contains adjacent vertices.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a way to represent a graph where each vertex has a list of its neighboring vertices. For an undirected graph with n vertices, we use a list of n lists: graph[i] contains all vertices j such that there is an edge between i and j. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs, using O(V+E) space. The intuition is that it directly captures connectivity, making it easy to iterate over neighbors during traversal algorithms like BFS. For example, if graph[0] = [1,2], it means vertex 0 is connected to vertices 1 and 2. Since the graph is undirected, if 0 is in graph[1], then 1 must be in graph[0]. This foundation is crucial for implementing BFS because we need to access neighbors quickly. Time to access neighbors of a vertex is O(degree(vertex)). This feeds into L4 as the data structure that BFS operates on. Common mistakes include not initializing the list correctly, forgetting to add edges in both directions for undirected graphs, or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: n = number of vertices, graph = [[] for _ in range(n)], for each edge (u,v): graph[u].append(v), graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph with 3 empty lists: [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): append 1 to graph[0], append 0 to graph[1]: graph = [[1],[0],[]]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): append 2 to graph[1], append 1 to graph[2]: graph = [[1],[0,2],[1]].

Step 1: Initialize graph with 4 empty lists: [[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): graph[0].append(1), graph[1].append(0): graph = [[1],[0],[],[]]. Step 3: Add edge (0,3): graph[0].append(3), graph[3].append(0): graph = [[1,3],[0],[],[0]]. Step 4: Add edge (1,2): graph[1].append(2), graph[2].append(1): graph = [[1,3],[0,2],[1],[0]]. Step 5: Add edge (2,3): graph[2].append(3), graph[3].append(2): graph = [[1,3],[0,2],[1,3],[0,2]].

approaches.name: Union-Find O(E log V)
approaches.summary: Use a disjoint-set data structure to group vertices into two sets based on edges, checking if any edge connects vertices within the same set to determine bipartiteness. It leverages union-find operations for efficient set management.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Bipartite Graph Using Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution using union-find to determine if an undirected graph is bipartite
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind using union-find for bipartiteness checking is that in a bipartite graph, vertices can be partitioned into two sets where all edges connect vertices from different sets. We can model this by creating 'enemy' relationships: if vertex u connects to vertex v, then u's allies cannot be v's allies. We maintain two groups for each vertex: its own group and its enemy group. For each edge (u, v), we check if u and v are already in the same group (which would violate bipartiteness). If not, we union u with v's enemy group and v with u's enemy group. This ensures they're placed in opposite partitions. The algorithm processes all edges, returning false immediately if any edge connects vertices within the same group, otherwise true after processing all edges. Time complexity is O(E log V) due to union-find operations with path compression and union by rank. Space complexity is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete bipartite checking logic using the union-find structure. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize enemy groups, not handling the case where a vertex has no enemy yet, and incorrect union logic. Real code pattern: Initialize parent and rank arrays for 2V elements (each vertex has a regular and enemy slot). For each edge, find roots of u and v. If same root, return false. Otherwise, union u with v's enemy and v with u's enemy.

examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7] (0-3: vertices, 4-7: enemy slots), rank=[0]*8. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1 (different). Union(0,5): parent[0]=5. Union(1,4): parent[1]=4. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=4, find(2)=2 (different). Union(1,6): parent[4]=6. Union(2,5): parent[2]=5. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=5, find(3)=3 (different). Union(2,7): parent[5]=7. Union(3,6): parent[3]=6. All edges processed, return true.

Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4,5], rank=[0]*6. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1. Union(0,4): parent[0]=4. Union(1,3): parent[1]=3. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=3, find(2)=2. Union(1,5): parent[3]=5. Union(2,4): parent[2]=4. Edge [0,2]: find(0)=4, find(2)=4 (same root), return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find with Enemy Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement union-find operations while maintaining enemy relationships for bipartite checking
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine extends basic union-find to handle the bipartite graph requirement by tracking 'enemy' groups. The intuition is that each vertex needs two representations: its current group and its opposing group (enemy). When we process an edge between u and v, we need to ensure they're in opposite groups. We achieve this by unioning u with v's enemy and v with u's enemy. The algorithm maintains parent and rank arrays of size 2n, where indices 0 to n-1 represent vertices and n to 2n-1 represent their enemy slots. For each edge, we find the roots of u and v. If they're the same, we've found a conflict. Otherwise, we union u with (v+n) and v with (u+n). This ensures the bipartite property. Time complexity is O(log V) per operation with union by rank and path compression. Space is O(V). This feeds into L0 by providing the core data structure operations needed for the complete solution. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing for enemy slots and not updating ranks properly during union. Real code pattern: find(x) with path compression, union(x,y) with rank optimization, and check edge(u,v) using the described logic.

examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4,5], rank=[0]*6. Process edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1. Union(0,4): parent[0]=4. Union(1,3): parent[1]=3. No conflict, return true.

Initialize arrays. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1. Union(0,4): parent[0]=4. Union(1,3): parent[1]=3. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=3, find(2)=2. Union(1,5): parent[3]=5. Union(2,4): parent[2]=4. Edge [0,2]: find(0)=4, find(2)=4 (same), detect conflict, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bipartite Checking with Disjoint Sets
ladders.desc: The central technique of using disjoint sets to partition vertices into two groups
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that bipartite graphs can be checked by maintaining two disjoint sets representing the two partitions. For each edge (u, v), we ensure u and v belong to different sets. If we ever find an edge connecting vertices in the same set, the graph is not bipartite. We implement this using a union-find data structure. The algorithm initializes a disjoint set for all vertices. For each edge, if find(u) == find(v), return false (conflict). Otherwise, we need to place u and v in opposite sets. We achieve this by unioning u with all vertices that must be in the opposite set of v, and vice versa. This creates the two partitions dynamically. The intuition is that edges create constraints: if u connects to v, then u cannot be in the same set as v. By propagating these constraints through unions, we build the partitions. Time complexity is O(E log V) for processing edges with union-find operations. Space is O(V). This connects to L1 by providing the logical foundation for the enemy tracking mechanism. Common mistakes include not initializing sets properly and incorrect conflict detection. Real code pattern: Initialize DSU for n vertices. For each edge (u,v): if same set, return false; else, union u with opposite set of v.

examples.trace: Initialize sets: {0}, {1}, {2}, {3}. Edge [0,1]: different sets, union 0's set with opposite of 1. New sets: {0,2}, {1,3}. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=3, find(2)=2 (different), union appropriately. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=2, find(3)=3 (different), union. Final partitions valid, return true.

Initialize sets: {0}, {1}, {2}. Edge [0,1]: different, union. New sets: {0,2}, {1}. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=1, find(2)=2 (different), union. Edge [0,2]: find(0)=0, find(2)=0 (same set), conflict detected, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Disjoint Set Union (DSU) Data Structure
ladders.desc: Implement the union-find data structure with path compression and union by rank
ladders.explanation: The disjoint set union (DSU) data structure, also known as union-find, maintains a collection of disjoint sets and supports two operations: find (determine which set an element belongs to) and union (merge two sets). It's crucial for efficient bipartite checking. The intuition is to represent each set as a tree, where each node points to its parent, and the root represents the set identifier. Find operation traverses to the root, using path compression to flatten the tree for future efficiency. Union operation merges two sets by attaching the root of one tree to the root of another, using union by rank (attaching smaller tree to larger) to keep trees balanced. This ensures near-constant time amortized operations. For n elements, time complexity is O(α(n)) per operation, where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant). Space is O(n) for parent and rank arrays. This connects to L2 by providing the efficient set management needed for bipartite checking. Common mistakes include not initializing parent to self, incorrect path compression, and forgetting rank updates. Real code pattern: class DSU with __init__(n), find(x) with path compression, union(x,y) with rank comparison.

examples.input: n=5, operations=[union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(2), union(1,3), find(0)]
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0]*5. union(0,1): rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, attach 0 to 1, parent[0]=1, rank[1]=1. union(2,3): parent[2]=3, rank[3]=1. find(0): path 0->1, return 1. find(2): path 2->3, return 3. union(1,3): rank[1]=1, rank[3]=1, attach 3 to 1, parent[3]=1, rank[1]=2. find(0): path 0->1, return 1.

examples.input: n=4, operations=[union(0,2), union(1,3), find(0), find(1), union(2,3), find(0)]
examples.trace: Initialize arrays. union(0,2): parent[0]=2, rank[2]=1. union(1,3): parent[1]=3, rank[3]=1. find(0): return 2. find(1): return 3. union(2,3): rank[2]=1, rank[3]=1, attach 3 to 2, parent[3]=2, rank[2]=2. find(0): return 2.

testCases.input: n=6, operations=[union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), find(2)]
n=7, operations=[union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,5), find(4)]
n=8, operations=[union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(1,3), union(5,7), find(6)]
n=9, operations=[union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(1,3), union(5,7), union(3,7), find(0)]
n=10, operations=[union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(8,9), union(1,3), union(5,7), union(3,9), find(8)]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation in DSU with path compression optimization
ladders.explanation: The find operation determines the representative (root) of the set containing a given element. With path compression, after finding the root, we update all nodes along the path to point directly to the root, flattening the tree for future efficiency. The intuition is that by compressing paths, we reduce the height of the tree, making subsequent finds faster. The algorithm is recursive or iterative: traverse parent pointers until reaching a node that is its own parent (the root), then update parents during backtracking. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to compression. Space is O(1) extra per call. This connects to L3 as a core component of the DSU data structure. Common mistakes include infinite recursion without base case, not updating parent pointers correctly, and using non-optimized linear search. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x].

examples.trace: Start at 0: parent[0]=1. Find(1): parent[1]=2. Find(2): parent[2]=2 (root). Backtrack: set parent[1]=2, return 2. Backtrack: set parent[0]=2, return 2. Final parent=[2,2,2,3,4].

Start at 0: parent[0]=1. Find(1): parent[1]=2. Find(2): parent[2]=3. Find(3): parent[3]=3 (root). Backtrack: set parent[2]=3, return 3. Backtrack: set parent[1]=3, return 3. Backtrack: set parent[0]=3, return 3. Final parent=[3,3,3,3,4].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand graphs as collections of vertices and edges, and how to traverse them
ladders.explanation: A graph is a data structure consisting of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). For bipartite checking, we typically use an undirected graph where edges have no direction. Graphs can be represented as adjacency lists (list of lists where each index has neighbors) or adjacency matrices. Basic traversal involves visiting vertices, often starting from a source and exploring connected vertices. In the context of bipartite checking, we need to process all edges to check constraints. The intuition is that graphs model relationships, and traversal helps inspect these relationships. For n vertices and m edges, time to build adjacency list is O(m), space is O(n+m). This connects to L4 by providing the foundational graph knowledge needed for DSU applications. Common mistakes include incorrect edge representation, forgetting that graphs can be disconnected, and off-by-one errors in vertex indexing. Real code pattern: Build adjacency list from edges: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Initialize adj = [[], [], []]. Edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0], add 0 to adj[1]. Edge [1,2]: add 2 to adj[1], add 1 to adj[2]. Final: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1].
Initialize adj = [[], [], [], []]. Edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0], add 0 to adj[1]. Edge [2,3]: add 3 to adj[2], add 2 to adj[3]. Final: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0], adj[2]=[3], adj[3]=[2].
